caveats: a: BLS occupational shares are for four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than this six-digit industry.
a: NIST use cases show technical applicability and adoption, not wage-weighted task exposure in packaging-film plants.
a: The injected compensation-to-receipts ratio is low and based on mismatched QCEW 2024 and SUSB 2022 vintages, but it is not used to set this task-share primitive.
rho: Manufacturing-wide survey adoption includes simple chatbots and does not measure the fraction of exposed labor opportunity actually implemented.
rho: Implementation rates may differ sharply between modern multi-layer extrusion assets and older lines with limited sensor and data infrastructure.
e: No source directly measures lens eligibility among estimated LMM firms.
e: The industry includes both scaled integrated producers and independent converters, and public-company product mixes do not reveal the LMM eligibility distribution.
e: The injected firm count is an estimate derived from broader size-distribution data.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and sizes, not NAICS 326112 or the LMM EBITDA band.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed transactions, and the source does not isolate qualifying control transfers.
s5: Corporate carve-outs and internal restructurings must be excluded even if plants change reporting ownership.
q: A large public producer's contracting and competitive disclosures may not match LMM firms' bargaining power.
q: Retention varies by differentiated barrier film, commodity converter film, contract duration, and customer concentration.
q: The range excludes demand loss, which is represented in d5 and o.
d5: BLS output covers all plastics products and a ten-year horizon, not this six-digit industry's five-year physical demand.
d5: Public-company end-market statements span products outside NAICS 326112.
d5: California policy is not a national forecast, but it signals a material regulatory and design pathway that may spread.
o: Elimination of plastic packaging quantity is principally captured in d5; o only captures whether surviving demand still needs this kind of external accountable operator.
o: The boundary between material substitution and operator substitution can be ambiguous for integrated refill and reuse systems.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Plastics Packaging Film and Sheet (including Laminated) Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring and repeat B2B sales of specified film or sheet, typically by roll, weight, or production order, to converters, packagers, and consumer-product supply chains; price resets and resin-index or other contractual mechanisms can transmit input-cost changes.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, shells, non-control financing vehicles, plastics bags and pouches, nonpackaging film and sheet, rigid packaging, and paper-foil-plastic laminating classified outside NAICS 326112.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers converting plastics resins into flexible packaging film and packaging sheet for recurring or repeat external customers, including repeat converter-film, food, consumer, stretch, and shrink-film programs.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 326100 workforce, where production occupations are 58.94% of employment and extrusion operators alone are 4.83%; NIST identifies AI use in inspection, maintenance, planning, inventory, documentation, and forecasting. I map high exposure to office, planning, quality-analysis, documentation, and selected vision-inspection tasks, moderate exposure to supervision and maintenance diagnostics, and low exposure to hands-on line tending and material movement. Packaging-film plants are likely more continuous-line and production-heavy than all plastics products, so the broader-industry bridge may overstate exposure.
caveats: BLS occupational shares are for four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than this six-digit industry.
NIST use cases show technical applicability and adoption, not wage-weighted task exposure in packaging-film plants.
The injected compensation-to-receipts ratio is low and based on mismatched QCEW 2024 and SUSB 2022 vintages, but it is not used to set this task-share primitive.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupational staffing table for NAICS 326112, paired with task-level time studies from packaging-film plants showing current automation and labor hours in planning, quality, maintenance, customer service, and line operation.
a.rationale: Most wages support physical extrusion, converting, setup, maintenance, handling, and accountable quality work, constraining substitution. The exposed portion is concentrated in production planning, sales administration, purchasing, quality records and analysis, troubleshooting support, predictive maintenance, and machine-vision inspection.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader plastics product manufacturing from 190.7 in 2024 to 212.4 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2034, equivalent to roughly 1.1% annual growth; a five-year continuation would imply about 1.056. I lower the base for packaging film and sheet because California's rules target less single-use plastic and substitution toward reuse or refill, while stable food, beverage, healthcare, and personal-care end markets and packaging's protection and shelf-life functions support demand. The broad-plastics proxy is likely upward biased for single-use packaging film where regulation is more acute.
caveats: BLS output covers all plastics products and a ten-year horizon, not this six-digit industry's five-year physical demand.
Public-company end-market statements span products outside NAICS 326112.
California policy is not a national forecast, but it signals a material regulatory and design pathway that may spread.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year volume forecast for US plastic packaging film and sheet by application and material, stated in constant-gauge equivalent units and incorporating enacted EPR, reduction, recycled-content, and reuse mandates by state.
d5.rationale: Current flexible packaging protects goods, extends shelf life, and serves stable consumer categories, supporting repeat demand. Lightweighting, material redesign, recycled-content constraints, EPR costs, reuse systems, paper or other substrate substitution, and customer sustainability goals can reduce constant-quality demand for the current plastic film basket.

caveats: No source directly measures lens eligibility among estimated LMM firms.
The industry includes both scaled integrated producers and independent converters, and public-company product mixes do not reveal the LMM eligibility distribution.
The injected firm count is an estimate derived from broader size-distribution data.
e.change_evidence: A plant-by-plant NAICS 326112 business registry enriched with ownership, customer type, recurring-order share, captive status, normalized EBITDA, and stand-alone financials.
e.rationale: Most independent film and sheet manufacturers in the EBITDA band should sell repeat production externally and therefore fit the lens. Ineligible firms include captive plants, operations dominated by one-off or nonrecurring activity, misclassified adjacent products, businesses without transferable stand-alone operations, and firms whose economics fall outside the stated band despite the dataset estimate.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 326112 ($1,869,224,561) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 326112 ($16,566,149,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $486,224 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 326112); class <5: 59 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,535,153 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $224,900 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,047,069 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $299,896 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,901,906 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $718,129 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 101 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,497,752 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,563,421 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $73,909,115 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,827,685 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $236,640,044 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,667,767 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 101

caveats: Elimination of plastic packaging quantity is principally captured in d5; o only captures whether surviving demand still needs this kind of external accountable operator.
The boundary between material substitution and operator substitution can be ambiguous for integrated refill and reuse systems.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and capacity announcements quantifying vertical integration, on-site or closed-loop production, reuse-system displacement, and film volumes eliminated rather than shifted among external suppliers.
o.rationale: Demand that remains for physical packaging film and sheet still requires an accountable manufacturer to convert resin, control thickness and barrier performance, maintain equipment, certify quality, and deliver rolls. Software can improve those workflows but cannot self-serve the physical product; the downside allows for customers vertically integrating or eliminating portions of the current basket through reuse and alternative packaging systems.

caveats: A large public producer's contracting and competitive disclosures may not match LMM firms' bargaining power.
Retention varies by differentiated barrier film, commodity converter film, contract duration, and customer concentration.
The range excludes demand loss, which is represented in d5 and o.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples and deal diligence showing resin-index clauses, renewal cadence, open-book costing, productivity-sharing terms, bid frequency, customer churn, and realized price/margin behavior after plant productivity projects.
q.rationale: An operator can retain part of implemented benefits during contract periods and through faster delivery, lower scrap, better quality, and improved uptime. Retention is constrained because resin changes are commonly passed through, customers can reprice at renewal, many products compete on price, service, quality, and timely supply, and customers may switch suppliers or substrates. Proprietary formulations, demanding specifications, customer qualification, and demonstrable service gains support the upper range.

rho.bridge: NIST reports manufacturing-wide AI use and expected growth, plus applicable deployments in quality, maintenance, planning, and document workflows. I translate that cross-manufacturing adoption evidence into the five-year implemented share of the narrower exposed opportunity, discounting for data quality, initial cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-system integration barriers. Because larger and digitally mature manufacturers are likely prominent in the source population, the proxy probably overstates implementation for LMM packaging-film firms.
caveats: Manufacturing-wide survey adoption includes simple chatbots and does not measure the fraction of exposed labor opportunity actually implemented.
Implementation rates may differ sharply between modern multi-layer extrusion assets and older lines with limited sensor and data infrastructure.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of LMM flexible-packaging or film converters documenting baseline systems, AI projects reaching production, realized labor hours avoided, implementation time, and project abandonment over three to five years.
rho.rationale: Well-bounded analytical and administrative workflows can be implemented within five years, and machine vision and predictive analytics fit continuous production. However, plant data fragmentation, validation requirements, equipment integration, workforce change, and the cost of line interruptions prevent full capture of exposed tasks.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of US employer-business owners plan to sell or transfer ownership within five years and that 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older. I use 22% as the broad employer-business anchor and allow a modest upward base adjustment for succession pressure and strategic interest in established manufacturing capacity. Capital intensity, environmental liabilities, and customer concentration can prevent an intended transfer, while industry consolidation can increase transfers; the net direction of proxy error is therefore ambiguous.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and sizes, not NAICS 326112 or the LMM EBITDA band.
Owner intentions are not completed transactions, and the source does not isolate qualifying control transfers.
Corporate carve-outs and internal restructurings must be excluded even if plants change reporting ownership.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed US control transactions for independent flexible-packaging film and sheet manufacturers, matched to the eligible-firm denominator and classified by owner age, buyer type, and whether the operating business remained transferable.
s5.rationale: A qualifying transfer is plausible where an owner has established extrusion assets, specifications, customer approvals, and repeat programs, but intentions do not always close and some plants transfer inside larger corporate reorganizations that do not qualify.

principal_driver: Repeat, specification-bound physical packaging demand creates a durable operating role and multiple bounded AI-enabled improvement workflows.
principal_weakness: Low labor intensity and strong customer repricing, substrate competition, and plastic-reduction pressure limit the amount and durability of captured AI benefit.
